أَفَمِنْ هَـٰذَا ٱلْحَدِيثِ تَعْجَبُونَ (٥٩)
Will you then wonder at this discourse, (59)
وَتَضْحَكُونَ وَلَا تَبْكُونَ (٦٠)
and laugh and not weep, (60)
وَأَنتُمْ سَـٰمِدُونَ (٦١)
while you remain heedless?! (61)
فَٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِلَّهِ وَٱعْبُدُوا۟ ۩ (٦٢)
So prostrate yourselves to Allah and worship Him! (62)

(Commentary)

The context of the nine verses situated at the beginning of this section corroborates what has been reported regarding the occasions of revelation (شأن النزول): that a man from among the Muslims used to spend his wealth in the way of Allah, but some people blamed him for his excessive spending, cautioned him, and frightened him with the prospect of the exhaustion of his wealth and poverty. One of them even guaranteed to bear his sins and burdens, so the man desisted from spending; thus, these verses were revealed.

By addressing this story and relaying what He relayed from the scrolls (صحف) of Abraham and Moses (عليه السلام), the Glorified has pointed toward an explanation of the truth regarding this matter. He also points toward the manifest truth concerning the falsehoods of the polytheists mentioned in the previous section—namely, their claim that they only worship idols because they are images of the angels, who are the daughters of Allah, and that they worship them so they might intercede for them with Allah, the Glorified. The preceding verses have already invalidated these claims in the clearest possible manner.

These verses have clarified the true nature of Lordship (الربوبية) and Divinity (الألوهية) — the absolute right of Allah alone to be worshipped and to manage creation — which is that creation (الخلق) and governance (التدبير) belong to Allah, the Glorified; to Him all such matters return. He created what He created and governed what He governed through a creation and governance that necessitates another life (نشأة أخرى) in which there is recompense for the disbeliever, the believer, the criminal, and the God-conscious (المتقي). Among its requirements is the legislation of religion (تشريع الدين) and the directing of religious obligations (التكاليف), which He has indeed done. Among its evidences is the destruction of those He destroyed from the past tyrannical nations, such as the people of Noah, ‘Ad, Thamud, and the Overturned Cities (المؤتفكة).

Then, the Glorified followed what He relayed from the scrolls of the two noble Prophets by drawing attention to the fact that this warner is one of the former warners who have passed, and that the Hour is near. He addressed them with the command to prostrate to Allah and to worship Him, and with this, the Surah concludes.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَرَأَيْتَ الَّذِي تَوَلَّى وَأَعْطى قَلِيلاً وَأَكْدى — "Did you see him who turned away, and gave a little, and then stinted?" (53:33-34). Turning away (التولي) is to avert oneself; what is meant here, by the context of the following verse, is turning away from spending in the way of Allah. Giving (الإعطاء) refers to spending, and stinting (الإكداء) refers to cutting off the gift. The derivation in His saying "Did you see" (أَفَرَأَيْتَ) is based on what we previously mentioned regarding the content of these verses branching out from what preceded them.

The meaning is: Tell me about the one who turned away from spending, gave a small amount of wealth, and then withheld after that with the utmost stinginess.

His saying, the Exalted: أَعِنْدَهُ عِلْمُ الْغَيْبِ فَهو يَرى — "Does he have the knowledge of the Unseen, so that he sees?" (53:35). The pronouns refer to the one who turned away, and the interrogation is for denunciation (الإنكار). The meaning is: Does he know the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — such that it results in him knowing that his companion will bear his sins for him and be punished in his place on the Day of Resurrection if he deserves punishment? This is how they have interpreted it.

However, it appears that what is meant is the denial of his knowledge regarding what is hidden from him of his future state in this world. The meaning would be: Does he know the Unseen such that he knows that if he were to spend and continue spending, his wealth would be exhausted and he would be afflicted with poverty? As for the bearing of sins and punishment, that is addressed by His coming saying: "that no bearer shall bear another’s burden."

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ لَمْ يُنَبَّأْ بِما فِي صُحُفِ مُوسى وَإِبْراهِيمَ الَّذِي وَفَّى — "Has he not been informed of what is in the manuscripts of Moses, and of Abraham, who fulfilled [his duty]?" (53:36-37). The scrolls (صحف) of Moses are the Torah, and the scrolls of Abraham are the Book revealed to him. The plural is used to indicate its abundance due to the multiplicity of its parts.

Fulfillment (التوفية) is the performance of a right in its entirety and perfection. His (peace be upon him) fulfillment was his performance of the rights of servitude (العبودية) — the state of total submission and devotion to Allah — in the most complete and profound manner. The Exalted said: وَإِذِ ابْتَلى إِبْراهِيمَ رَبُّهُ بِكَلِماتٍ فَأَتَمَّهُنَّ — "And when his Lord tested Abraham with certain words, and he fulfilled them..." (2:124).

What Allah, the Glorified, relates in the following verses from the scrolls of Abraham and Moses (peace be upon them), even if not mentioned in the Quran under the title of being from their scrolls prior to these verses, is nonetheless mentioned under the titles of rulings, exhortations, stories, and lessons. Thus, the meaning of the two verses is: Has he not been informed of these matters, which are present in the scrolls of Abraham and Moses?

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَّا تَزِرُ وازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرى — "that no bearer shall bear another’s burden" (53:38). The burden (الوزْر) is a heavy weight, and its usage is frequent regarding sin (الإثم). The bearer (الوازرة) is the soul (النفس) whose nature is to carry sin. The verse is an explanation of what is in the scrolls of Abraham and Moses (peace be upon them), as are the rest of the verses introduced by "that" (أن) until the completion of seventeen verses.

The meaning is: What is in their scrolls is that no soul shall carry the sin of another soul; that is, no soul becomes sinful by the sin belonging to another, nor is any soul taken to task for the sin of another.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْ لَيْسَ لِلْإِنْسانِ إِلَّا ما سَعى — "and that nothing belongs to man except what he strives for" (53:39). Al-Raghib said: "Striving (السعي) is fast walking, which is less than running. It is used for diligence in a matter, whether good or evil. The Exalted said: وَسَعى فِي خَرابِها — '...and strives to ruin them' (2:114)." End quote. Its use for diligence in action is a metaphorical (استعاري) usage.

The meaning of the preposition "for" (اللام) in His saying "for man" (لِلْإِنْسانِ) refers to true ownership (الملك الحقيقي) which subsists through its owner, remaining as long as he remains, accompanying him and never naturally departing from him. This is what a human acquires through righteous deeds (صالح العمل) or wicked ones, whether good or evil. As for what a human sees as being owned by himself within the social context—such as wealth, children, status, and other vanities and ornaments of the worldly life—all of that is conventional, illusory ownership (الملك الاعتباري الوهمي) that accompanies the human as long as he is in the abode of delusion (دار الغرور) and which he leaves behind when he intends to transition to the abode of eternity (دار الخلود) and the world of the Hereafter.

Thus, the meaning is: Man does not possess any ownership whose effect—whether good or evil, benefit or harm—truly returns to him, except for that which he was diligent in performing of deeds. He possesses what subsists through his own action. As for the action performed by another, its effect, whether good or evil, does not attach to the human.

As for benefiting from the intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — of the intercessors on the Day of Resurrection for those who committed major sins, they have a "beautiful striving" in that regard, as they entered the fold of faith in Allah and His signs. Similarly, the believer's benefiting after his death from the prayers of the believers for his forgiveness, and the righteous deeds whose rewards are gifted to him, are linked to his striving in entering the ranks of the believers, increasing their numbers, and supporting their faith, the effects of which include the righteous deeds they perform.

Likewise, whoever establishes a good sunnah (سنة حسنة) — a commendable tradition or practice — has its reward and the reward of those who act upon it. And whoever establishes an evil sunnah has its burden and the burden of those who act upon it until the Day of Resurrection. This is because he has a "striving" in their action, as he established the sunnah and used it as a means to their actions, as previously mentioned in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: وَنَكْتُبُ ما قَدَّمُوا وَآثارَهُمْ — "...and We write what they have sent ahead and their footsteps" (36:12). Useful discourse on this matter has also preceded in the commentary on His saying: وَلْيَخْشَ الَّذِينَ لَوْ تَرَكُوا مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّةً ضِعافاً خافُوا عَلَيْهِمْ — "Let those fear who, if they were to leave behind weak offspring, would be concerned for them" (4:9), and the commentary on His saying: لِيَمِيزَ اللهُ الْخَبِيثَ مِنَ الطَّيِّبِ — "...so that Allah may separate the bad from the good" (8:37).

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنَّ سَعْيَهُ سَوْفَ يُرى — "and that his striving will soon be seen" (53:40). What is meant by striving (السعي) is the deed he strove in, and by seeing (الرؤية) is witnessing (المشاهدة). The time of witnessing is the Day of Resurrection, as evidenced by its being followed by recompense. Thus, the verse is close in meaning to His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُحْضَراً وَما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ سُوءٍ — "The day when every soul will find present whatever good it has done; and as for the evil it has done..." (3:30), and His saying: يَوْمَئِذٍ يَصْدُرُ النَّاسُ أَشْتاتاً لِيُرَوْا أَعْمالَهُمْ فَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ خَيْراً يَرَهُ وَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ شَرًّا يَرَهُ — "On that day mankind will issue forth in various groups to be shown their deeds. So whoever does an atom’s weight of good will see it, and whoever does an atom’s weight of evil will see it" (99:6-8).

The use of His saying "will be seen" (سَوْفَ يُرى) in the passive voice is not without an indication that there are those who will witness the deed other than the one who performed it.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ يُجْزاهُ الْجَزاءَ الْأَوْفى — "then he will be rewarded for it with the fullest reward" (53:41). Fullness (الوفاء) means completeness, because a complete thing fulfills all that is required of its attributes. The fullest reward (الجزاء الأوفى) is the most complete reward.

The pronoun in "rewarded for it" (يُجْزاهُ) refers to the striving, which is the deed. The meaning is: Then the human will be rewarded for his deed—that is, by his deed—with the most complete reward.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنَّ إِلى رَبِّكَ الْمُنْتَهى — "and that the terminus is toward your Lord" (53:42). The terminus (المنتهى) is a mim-infinitive meaning "the ending" (الانتهاء). It has been used absolutely, thus signifying absolute ending. There is nothing in existence except that it ends, in its existence and the effects of its existence, to Allah, the Glorified, whether without a medium or through a medium. Nor is there any matter of governance (التدبير) or the prevailing system, whether particular or universal, except that it ends with Him, the Glorified. For the governance prevailing between things is nothing but the prevailing relations between them that subsist through them, and the Originator of things is the Originator of their relations who makes them prevail between them. Thus, the absolute terminus of everything is Allah, the Glorified.

The Exalted said: اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَهُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَكِيلٌ لَهُ مَقالِيدُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "Allah is the creator of all things, and He is guardian over all things. To Him belong the keys of the heavens and the earth" (39:62-63), and He said: أَلا لَهُ الْخَلْقُ وَالْأَمْرُ — "Look! All creation and command belong to Him" (7:54).

The verse establishes absolute Lordship (الربوبية المطلقة) for Allah, the Glorified, by ending every governance and all governance to Him. This includes the ending of things to Him in terms of the beginning, which is the origination (الفطر), and their ending to Him in terms of the return, which is the Resurrection (الحشر).

From what has preceded, the weakness of what has been said in the interpretation of the verse becomes apparent: that what is meant is the return of creation to Him, the Glorified, on the Day of Resurrection. Likewise, the claim that the meaning is that the terminus of the matter is toward the reward and punishment of your Lord; or that the meaning is that their terminus is toward the reckoning of your Lord; or that the meaning is that thoughts end at Him, the Glorified, and stop before Him. In all these interpretations, there is a restriction of the verse without a restrictor.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنَّهُ هُوَ أَضْحَكَ وَأَبْكى — "and that it is He who makes [one] laugh and weep" (53:43). This verse and those following it until the completion of twelve verses are an explanation of instances where creation and governance end with Allah, the Glorified.

The context in all these verses is one of exclusivity (الحصر), signifying the restriction of Lordship to Him, the Exalted, and the negation of any partner. The exclusivity in these instances is not contradicted by the mediation of other causes, whether natural or non-natural—such as the mediation of joy and sadness, and the organs of laughter and weeping in the human for the realization of laughter and weeping. Similarly, the mediation of appropriate natural and non-natural causes in giving life, causing death, creating the two pairs, giving wealth and possessions, and destroying the perished nations. This is because since these causes are subjected to the command of Allah, not independent in themselves nor severed from what is above them, their existences, the effects of their existences, and what results from them belong to Allah alone; no one shares with Him in that.

Thus, the meaning of His saying: "and that it is He who makes [one] laugh and weep" is that He, the Exalted, is the one who brought about laughter in the one who laughs and brought about weeping in the one who weeps, and none other than He, the Exalted.

There is no contradiction between the ending of laughter and weeping in their existence to Allah, the Glorified, and their attribution to the human and his being characterized by them. This is because the attribution of the act to the human is by his performing it, while the attribution of the act to Him, the Exalted, is by His bringing it into existence (الإيجاد)—and how great is the difference between the two!

Nor does the attachment of the Divine Will (الإرادة الإلهية) to the laughter of a human, for example, necessitate the nullification of the human's will to laugh or its failure to have an effect. This is because the Divine Will did not attach to absolute laughter in whatever manner it might occur; rather, it attached to intentional, voluntary laughter insofar as it issues from the human's will and choice. Thus, the human's will is a cause for his laughter in longitudinal alignment (في طول) with Allah's will, not in horizontal alignment (في عرض) with it, such that they would conflict or not coexist. Thus, we are not forced to say that the voluntary acts of humans are created by Allah and the human has no part in them—as the Determinist (الجبري) says—nor that they are created by the human and Allah, the Glorified, has no part in them—as the Mu'tazilite (المعتزلي) says.

From what has preceded, the invalidity of the statement of some becomes clear: that the meaning of the verse is that He created the powers of laughter and weeping. Or the statement of others: that the meaning is that He created joy and sadness. Or the statement of others: that the meaning is that He made the earth laugh with plants and made the sky weep with rain. Or the statement of others: that the meaning is that He made the people of Paradise laugh and the people of Hell weep.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنَّهُ هُوَ أَماتَ وَأَحْيا — "and that it is He who causes death and gives life" (53:44). The discussion regarding the attribution of death and life to other natural and non-natural causes, such as the angels, is the same as the discussion regarding the attribution of laughter and weeping to other than Him, the Exalted, while the bringing into existence is restricted to Him, the Exalted. The same applies to the matters mentioned in the following verses.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنَّهُ خَلَقَ الزَّوْجَيْنِ الذَّكَرَ وَالْأُنْثى مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ إِذا تُمْنى — "and that He created the two mates, the male and the female, from a drop of [seminal] fluid when it is emitted" (53:45-46). The drop (النطفة) is the fluid of the man and the woman from which the child is created. "Emitted" (أمنى) means the man poured the semen; it is also said to mean "proportioned." His saying "the male and the female" is an explanation of "the two mates."

It is said that the pronoun was not mentioned in this verse in the style of what preceded—"that it is He" (أنه هو)—because the attribution of the creation of the two mates to other than Him, the Exalted, is inconceivable.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنَّ عَلَيْهِ النَّشْأَةَ الْأُخْرى — "and that the second genesis lies with Him" (53:47). The second genesis (النشأة الأخرى) is the second creation, which is the abode of the Hereafter in which there is recompense. Its being "upon Him" (عليه), the Exalted, is His decree—a definitive decree. He has promised it and described Himself as one who does not break His promise.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنَّهُ هُوَ أَغْنى وَأَقْنى — "and that it is He who enriches and gives possession" (53:48). That is, He gave wealth and He gave possessions. Possession (القنية) refers to wealth that endures and remains by its own nature, such as a house, a garden, or livestock. Based on this, the mention of "gave possession" (أَقْنى) after "enriches" (أَغْنى) is an instance of addressing the specific after the general due to its value and nobility.

It is also said that enriching (الإغناء) is providing wealth, and giving possession (الإقناء) is making one satisfied with it. Some have said the meaning of the verse is that He is the one who enriches and makes poor.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنَّهُ هُوَ رَبُّ الشِّعْرى — "and that it is He who is the Lord of Sirius" (53:49). What is meant by Sirius (الشعرى) is Sirius the Yemenite, which is a bright star among the fixed stars to the east of the constellation Orion in the sky.

It is said that the tribes of Khuza'ah and Himyar used to worship this star. Among those who worshipped it was Abu Kabshah, one of the ancestors of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) from his mother's side. The polytheists used to call him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) "the son of Abu Kabshah" because of his opposition to them in religion, just as Abu Kabshah had opposed his people in the worship of Sirius.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنَّهُ أَهْلَكَ عاداً الْأُولى — "and that He destroyed the former ‘Ad" (53:50). These were the people of the Prophet Hud (peace be upon him). They were described as "the former" because there was a second ‘Ad who came after the former ‘Ad.

His saying, the Exalted: وَثَمُودَ فَما أَبْقى — "and Thamud, leaving no one [of them] spare" (53:51). These were the people of the Prophet Salih (peace be upon him). Allah destroyed the disbelievers among them to the last man, which is the meaning of His saying "leaving no one spare" (فَما أَبْقى). Otherwise, He, the Glorified, saved the believers among them from destruction, as He said: وَنَجَّيْنَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَكانُوا يَتَّقُونَ — "And We delivered those who had faith and were God-conscious" (41:18).

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَوْمَ نُوحٍ مِنْ قَبْلُ إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا هُمْ أَظْلَمَ وَأَطْغى — "and the people of Noah before [them]—indeed they were more unjust and more rebellious" (53:52). This is coordinated, like the one before it, with His saying "‘Ad." The insistence with emphasis on their being "more unjust and more rebellious" means more so than the two peoples, ‘Ad and Thamud, as suggested by the context. This is because they did not answer the call of Noah (peace be upon him) nor did they take heed of his exhortation for nearly a thousand years, and none believed with him except a very few.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْمُؤْتَفِكَةَ أَهْوى فَغَشَّاها ما غَشَّى — "and He overthrew the Overturned Cities, so there covered them that which covered" (53:53-54). It is said that the Overturned Cities (المؤتفكة) were the towns of the people of Lot; they were overturned (ائتفكت) with their inhabitants—that is, they were flipped. Overturning (الائتفاك) is flipping, and overthrowing (الأهواء) is causing to fall.

The meaning is: He caused the Overturned Cities to fall to the earth by flipping them and causing them to be swallowed by the earth, so there covered them and surrounded them of the punishment that which covered and surrounded them.

It is possible that what is meant by the Overturned Cities is more general than the towns of the people of Lot; namely, every town upon which punishment descended such that its people perished, leaving it a ruined, obliterated wasteland, its landmarks empty upon its foundations.

His saying, the Exalted: فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكَ تَتَمارى — "So which of your Lord’s bounties will you dispute?" (53:55). Bounties (الآلاء) is the plural of ila, meaning blessing (النعمة). Disputing (التماري) is doubting. The sentence is derived from what was previously mentioned of the acts attributed to Him, the Exalted.

The meaning is: If Allah, the Glorified, is the one who organized this wondrous system of creation and governance through making laugh and weep, causing death and giving life, creating and destroying, until the end of what was said—then which of your Lord's blessings do you doubt, and in which of them do you waver?

Instances such as making weep, causing death, and destroying tyrannical nations are counted as blessings of Allah, the Glorified, because of their role in the formation of the most perfect system (النظام الأتم) that prevails in the world, by which matters are driven in the stage of the completion of creation and the return of all to Allah, the Glorified.

The address in the verse is either to the one who turned away, gave a little, and then stinted, or to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in the manner of "I mean you, but listen, O neighbor." The interrogation is for denunciation.

His saying, the Exalted: هذا نَذِيرٌ مِنَ النُّذُرِ الْأُولى — "This is a warner from among the former warners" (53:56). It is said that warner (النذير) can be an infinitive meaning "warning" or an adjective meaning "one who warns," and its plural is nudhur (النذر) in both meanings. The demonstration "this" (هذا) refers to the Quran or the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

His saying, the Exalted: أَزِفَتِ الْآزِفَةُ — "The Imminent [Hour] has drawn near" (53:57). That is, the Resurrection has drawn near. The Imminent (الآزفة) is one of the names of the Resurrection. The Exalted said: وَأَنْذِرْهُمْ يَوْمَ الْآزِفَةِ — "Warn them of the Day of the Imminent [Hour]..." (40:18).

His saying, the Exalted: لَيْسَ لَها مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ كاشِفَةٌ — "None besides Allah can disclose it" (53:58). That is, no soul (نفس) can disclose it. What is meant by disclosure (الكشف) is the removal of the hardships and terrors within it. The meaning is: No soul is capable of removing the hardships and terrors within it unless Allah, the Glorified, discloses it.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَمِنْ هذَا الْحَدِيثِ تَعْجَبُونَ وَتَضْحَكُونَ وَلا تَبْكُونَ وَأَنْتُمْ سامِدُونَ — "Do you then marvel at this discourse? And do you laugh, and not weep, while you are negligent?" (53:59-61). The demonstration "this discourse" (هذا الحديث) refers to the preceding explanation. Negligence (السمود) is diversion (اللهو). The verse is derived from the preceding explanation, and the interrogation is for rebuke (التوبيخ).

The meaning is: If Allah is your Lord to whom every matter ends and with whom the second genesis lies, and the Resurrection is near and none besides Allah can disclose it—then it was incumbent upon you to weep for what you neglected regarding the side of Allah and for exposing yourselves to eternal wretchedness. Do you then marvel at this explanation which calls you to salvation out of denial, and do you laugh in mockery and not weep?

His saying, the Exalted: فَاسْجُدُوا لِلَّهِ وَاعْبُدُوا — "So prostrate yourselves before Allah and worship [Him]!" (53:62). This is another derivation from the preceding explanation. The meaning is: If it is so, then it is incumbent upon you to prostrate to Allah and worship Him, so that He may disclose from you that which none besides Him can disclose.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kashshaf: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَفَرَأَيْتَ الَّذِي تَوَلَّى etc., it is narrated that ‘Uthman used to give his wealth in charity. ‘Abdullah ibn Sa‘d ibn Abi Sarh—who was his brother through fosterage—said to him: "It is likely that nothing will remain for you." ‘Uthman said: "I have sins and transgressions, and I seek through what I do the pleasure of Allah, the Exalted, and I hope for His pardon." ‘Abdullah said: "Give me your she-camel with its saddle and I will bear all your sins for you." So he gave it to him and had it witnessed, and he withheld from giving; thus it was revealed. And the meaning of "turned away" (تَوَلَّى) is that he left his position on the day of Uhud, then ‘Uthman returned to what was better and more beautiful than that.

I say: The story is also mentioned in Majma‘ al-Bayan and attributed to Ibn ‘Abbas, al-Suddi, al-Kalbi, and a group of commentators. However, the application of "turned away" to his leaving his position on the day of Uhud is questionable, as the verses are Meccan.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Faryabi, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Mujahid regarding His saying: أَفَرَأَيْتَ الَّذِي تَوَلَّى: He said: "Al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah used to come to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and Abu Bakr and listen to what they said, and that was what he gave of himself—he gave his listening. وَأَكْدى (and stinted): He said: his giving was cut off. Regarding this was revealed: أَعِنْدَهُ عِلْمُ الْغَيْبِ (Does he have the knowledge of the Unseen). He said: The Unseen is the Quran; did he see falsehood in it and carry it out with his vision when he used to frequent the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and Abu Bakr?"

I say: You are well aware that the verses, in their apparent meaning, do not align with what he mentioned.

It is also narrated that they were revealed concerning al-‘As ibn Wa’il, and it is narrated that they were revealed concerning a man whose name was not mentioned.

In Tafsir al-Qummi: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَإِبْراهِيمَ الَّذِي وَفَّى: He said: "He fulfilled what Allah commanded him of commands and prohibitions, and the sacrifice of his son."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Ishaq ibn ‘Ammar from Abu Ibrahim (peace be upon him), he said: I asked him about a man who performs Hajj and designates his Hajj and ‘Umrah—or some of his circumambulation—for some of his family members while they are absent in another land? He said: I said: "Does that decrease his reward?" He said: "هي له ولصاحبه وله أجر سوى ذلك بما وصل" — "It is for him and for his companion, and he has a reward besides that for the kindness he showed." I said: "And if he is dead, does that reach him?" He said: "نعم حتى يكون مسخوطا عليه فيغفر له ـ أو يكون مضيقا عليه فيوسع له" — "Yes, even if he was an object of wrath, he is forgiven—or if he was in constraint, it is expanded for him." I said: "Does he know in his place that this was done for his sake?" He said: "نعم" — "Yes." I said: "And if he was a Nasibi (an enemy of the Ahl al-Bayt), does that benefit him?" He said: "نعم يخفف عنه" — "Yes, it is lightened for him."

I say: The subject of the narration is gifting the reward of the deed, not performing the deed as a proxy for the deceased.

In it, with its chain of transmission from ‘Abdullah ibn Sinan from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "يقول الله عز وجل للملك الموكل بالمؤمن إذا مرض : اكتب له ما كنت تكتب له في صحته ـ فإني أنا الذي صيرته في حبالي" — "Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says to the angel entrusted with the believer when he falls ill: 'Write for him what you used to write for him during his health—for I am the one who has placed him in My bonds (حبالي).'" (1)

In al-Khisal, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: "ليس يتبع الرجل بعد موته من الأجر إلا ثلاث خصال : صدقة أجراها في حياته ـ فهي تجري بعد موته إلى يوم القيامة ـ صدقة موقوفة لا تورث ، وسنة هدى سنها ـ وكان يعمل بها وعمل بها من بعده غيره ، وولد صالح يستغفر له" — "Nothing follows a man after his death of reward except three traits: a charity he initiated during his life—so it continues after his death until the Day of Resurrection—an endowed charity that is not inherited; a sunnah of guidance he established—which he used to act upon and others acted upon after him; and a righteous child who prays for his forgiveness."

I say: These three narrations—and there are very many narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) with this meaning—expand the meaning of striving (السعي) in His saying, the Exalted: "and that nothing belongs to man except what he strives for," as previously indicated.

In Usul al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission to Sulayman ibn Khalid, he said: Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "Allah says: وَأَنَّ إِلى رَبِّكَ الْمُنْتَهى (and that the terminus is toward your Lord). فإذا انتهى الكلام إلى الله فأمسكوا" — "So when the discourse reaches Allah, then desist."

I say: This is an instance of expanding the meaning of "ending" (الانتهاء).

In it, with its chain of transmission to Abu ‘Ubaydah al-Hadhdha’, he said: Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) said: "يا زياد إياك والخصومات فإنها تورث الشك ، وتحبط العمل ، وتردي صاحبها ، وعسى أن يتكلم بالشيء فلا يغفر له. أنه كان فيما مضى قوم تركوا علم ما وكلوا به ، وطلبوا علم ما كفوه حتى انتهى كلامهم إلى الله ـ فتحيروا حتى كان الرجل يدعى من بين يديه ـ فيجيب من خلفه ، ويدعى من خلفه فيجيب من بين يديه" — "O Ziyad, beware of disputations, for they bequeath doubt, nullify deeds, and ruin their practitioner; perhaps he might say something for which he is not forgiven. Indeed, in the past, there was a people who abandoned the knowledge they were entrusted with and sought the knowledge they were spared, until their discourse reached Allah—so they became bewildered, such that a man would be called from before him and he would answer from behind him, and be called from behind him and he would answer from before him." He said: In another narration: "حتى تاهوا في الأرض" — "...until they wandered lost in the earth."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abu al-Shaykh narrated from Abu Dharr, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "تفكروا في خلق الله ولا تفكروا في الله فتهلكوا" — "Reflect upon the creation of Allah and do not reflect upon Allah, lest you perish."

I say: There are many other narrations prohibiting reflection upon Allah, the Glorified, recorded in the collections of both groups (Sunni and Shi'a). The prohibition is advisory (إرشادي), pertaining to those who are not proficient in entering deep intellectual matters, such that their delving into them would be an exposure to eternal destruction.

In Tafsir al-Qummi: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنَّهُ هُوَ أَضْحَكَ وَأَبْكى: He said: "أبكى السماء بالمطر ، وأضحك الأرض بالنبات" — "He made the sky weep with rain, and made the earth laugh with plants."

I say: This is an instance of expanding the meaning of making weep and making laugh.

In al-Ma‘ani, with its chain of transmission to al-Sukuni from Ja‘far ibn Muhammad from his fathers (peace be upon them), he said: The Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَأَنَّهُ هُوَ أَغْنى وَأَقْنى: "أغنى كل إنسان بمعيشته ، وأرضاه بكسب يده" — "He enriched every human with his livelihood, and made him satisfied with the earning of his hand."

In Tafsir al-Qummi: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنَّهُ هُوَ رَبُّ الشِّعْرى: He said: "النجم في السماء يسمى الشعرى كانت قريش وقوم من العرب يعبدونه ، وهو نجم يطلع في آخر الليل" — "The star in the sky called Sirius; Quraysh and a group of the Arabs used to worship it, and it is a star that rises at the end of the night."

I say: It appears that his saying "it is a star that rises at the end of the night" is a definition of it according to the time the tradition was issued, which was in summer; otherwise, throughout the entire year, it covers all hours of the night and day.

In it: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَزِفَتِ الْآزِفَةُ: He said: "The Resurrection has drawn near."

In al-Majma‘: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَفَمِنْ هذَا الْحَدِيثِ تَعْجَبُونَ: "He means by 'discourse' (الحديث) the preceding reports."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: "When this verse was revealed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): أَفَمِنْ هذَا الْحَدِيثِ تَعْجَبُونَ وَتَضْحَكُونَ وَلا تَبْكُونَ, the Prophet was never seen laughing after that until he departed from the world."

(1) al-Hibalah: The bond or snare.
